lg.l: maṇikaṇṭu tāḻnta kuṭaiyaṉ ṟaḷarnta ceṅ kōlaṉ
There [in my, i.e., the Pāṇṭiya queen’s dream],
I would witness the trembling, sweet sound [of] the entrance gate,
The sceptre [of the king] having stayed fallen along with [his] paracol.
Look, friend! Apart from all the eight directions shaking,
I would watch, as the darkness swallows the sun. Look, friend!
Stars [with] harsh rays [= shooting stars] would fall [in] the hot daytime.
I would witness these things. Look, friend!
A prognosis: The straight sceptre and the white parasol
Having toppled upon the dense ground, will fall.
As the bells of the victorious entrance of the good king shiver,
The heart shivers [along with it]. [At] night, there are rainbows.
At daytime, [shooting] stars fall. All the eight directions vibrate.
There is a great sorrow that is coming. Let me tell the king.
As those who carry mirrors, those who carry jewels,
Those with jewels [of] glittering sapphires that shine,
Those carrying cloths, those who carry silks,
Those who carry ornate boxes of rich betel leaves,
Those who carry colour[-pastes], those who carry powders,
Those who carry the paste of deermusk, those with chaplets,
Those who carry garlands, those who carry chowries,
[And] those who carry incense, joining in [with] young people
[With] menial tasks that went along with hunchbacks, dwarves
And the mute, [all of them] surrounding [the palace],
As ladies with fragrant hair mixed with greys
[And] many [more] shared praises mixed with [good] words,
Saying, ‘May the great wife of the Pāṇṭiyaṉ who guards
The world here, [surrounded by] waters! live long!’, Having gone [to her],
As the retinue and the guards praised with words the royal procession,
As the great wife of the king, having gone, narrated the matter of the evil dream,
The king of the southerners with a chest that Tiru desires,
Sat upon a bed upon which a lion [figure] was raised.
[Kaṇṇaki], saying over here, ‘Hey, guardsman! Hey, guardsman!
Oh guardsman of him who deviated from the manners of sovereignty,
With a heart that cuts away good Karma, in which common sense
Has gone corrupt! You will make known, you will make known, that
There is a lady [with] a hand in which the pair of the tinkling anklet is held up;
A lady who has lost [her] husband; one who is inside the gates!
The gatekeeper, saying, ‘Live long, oh king of Koṟkai, live long!
Oh leader of the southern mountains, live long!
Oh Ceḻiyaṉ, live long! Pañcavaṉ, who does not think with deceit, live long!
She is not Koṟṟavai, [with] broad hands [with] a victorious spear,
The [lady like] a young creeper, who mounted the throne [that was]
The head [at the] nape [of Mahiṣāsura, with] fresh cuts from which
Blood that rose, [she] having killed [him], did not stop [flowing].
Nor is she the lady who tore [with] great strength [the demon] Tārukaṉ,
[As] Kāḷi who rejoiced in/rose from the forest that possesses
Cīr and Aṇaṅku spirits, [Kāḷi] who graced, having witnessed
The dancing of the Lord — the lady who is the youngest of six!
It seems she is destroyed. It seems she is angered.
She is one with a hand that has lifted an anklet made of gold.
She who is inside the entrance has lost her husband.
She who is inside the entrance has lost her husband.’
[But the king] said, ‘But let her come here, bring [her] here.’
[The doorman], having come [back] to the entrance,
Showing [her] the palace, when she who approached
The king of the palace had arrived, [the king] saying,
‘Oh you with eyes that overflow [with] tears! You who come
Before me! Who could you be, oh you young creeper?’
[Kaṇṇaki] saying, ‘Thoughtless king! I have something to say.
Apart from [it] being of him who pulled away, having seen,
The great sorrow of a [mere] bird, so that the immortals
Of faultless excellence were amazed, Pukār of a great name
Of him who trampled his own son who was rare to obtain,
As [his] heart burned [for] the tears that entered the jewels
[That were] the ends [of the eyes] of a cow, as the middle-tongue
Of the bell [at] the entrance door shook, is my city! In that town,
[Him] being the son of the trader Mācāttuvaṉ of a great family
In which unadmonishing fame shone, wishing a new life [in Maturai],
As ripening Karma goaded [him], oh king [with] anklets that surround [you],
[He] having entered your city, wishing to sell the anklet [on] my foot,
The name Kaṇṇaki - of the wife of Kōvalaṉ who fell upon
The murderous ground because of you - is my name!’
[The king] saying, ‘Oh fearful, female deity! The killing of a thief
Is not [a sign of] a cruel sceptre [of justice]. Watch the victory
Of the white spear [of justice]!’, [Kaṇṇaki of] sweet words said,
‘Oh king of Koṟkai who thinks not of noble matters! The golden anklet
[On] my foot is of tinkling bits possessing gems!’ [The king] saying,
‘Oh [girl] of straight, sharp, good words! The anklet we possess
Is of tinkling bits possessing pearls!’ [Kaṇṇaki] saying ‘Give [it here]’,
[The king] having given [it], placing [it] in front of her,
As Kaṇṇaki broke the beautiful, tinkling anklet of gems [from her] foot,
The gems flew into the face of the king. [He] having seen the gems,
He with a parasol that hung low, he of the straight sceptre [of justice]
That [now] drooped, saying, ‘Am I really a king who heeded the words
Of a goldsmith? In fact, I am the thief! My principles of guarding
The southern soils that protect humanity have failed. May my life be lost!’
Saying [thus], he fell, having fainted. The great royal wife of the southern one,
Trembling as one who became unravelled [with grief], saying,
‘There is nothing [left] to show to those who have lost their husband!’
Having worshipped [his] paired feet, [that woman of] inexperienced words
